title: ADCN: Open Biomechanics Research, U.S. Sport Policy, News
text:
The Athletes Data Community Newsletter (ADCN) is produced by Brad Stenger, a researcher studying applied privacy technologies in the context of athletes’ data at the University of Vermont. The newsletter covers the athletes’ health and performance data pipeline, from collection to management to reporting, and the factors affecting athletes’ data. Thanks for reading!

University Research in Open Biomechanics
The KneeHub project just published an update to a biomechanics models benchmarking study first published in 2021. KneeHub was conceived as a way to determine what’s art and what’s science in the process of constructing biomechanical models. Five teams independently developed knee biomechanics models using their preferred workflow. Different workflows produced different predictions for the same scenarios. But all of the differences in the models’ results could be explained. “Our most important conclusion,” the authors wrote, is “the ability of [biomechanics] models to predict beyond the conditions used to calibrate them.”
The result offers useful validation for personalized approaches to neuromuscular rehabilitation. Another recent advance comes from B.J. Fregly’s group at Rice University. The Neuromusculoskeletal Modeling (NMSM) Pipeline software “allows researchers to work in collaboration with clinicians to construct personalized neuromusculoskeletal computer models of individual patients (i.e., digital twins)” then use those models for surgical or neuromuscular rehabilitation treatments. The digital twin gives clinicians the opportunity to explore and test treatment paths, leading to greater confidence in expected outcomes.
Researchers at the University of California-San Diego combine biomechanical models of athletic training movements with an AI that informs physiological and biomuscular limitation to create models (and videos) that can differentiate risk in athletes’ training movements. The work is early-stage and the research team plans to add to the number of training movements and incorporate personalization.
This is, I think, what you want to see for progress in Open computational biomechanics. The innovations are not just data, or software, or process. University researchers are pushing in all of those directions at the same time with their projects.


U.S. Sport Policy Coming Through Congress (Or Not)

The last two weeks had more mentions of college athlete rights than the last two years. It’s a low bar. There hasn’t been very many mentions.
The change in public sports vocabulary comes, in large part, from new federal legislation timed to coincide with the annual NCAA Convention held in Washington DC and the College Football Playoff Title game between Miami and Indiana (not in Washington DC). The noise in government circles was captured by The Hill’s Dominick Mastrangelo who wrote, “Lawmakers in both parties are outraged at the NCAA and signaling intervention from Congress on athlete compensation and revenue sharing is inevitable.”
More of the cacophony around college sports legislation suggests the opposite, that Congressional intervention in U.S. college sports is a work in progress. Nothing here is actually inevitable, or even seems close to resolution. Distrust for the NCAA is high, making the anti-trust excemption that is central to initial legislation difficult to justify, especially since anti-trust and court judgements are the main recourse for college student-athletes who lack for the rights that employees have (just for being employees).
Sports law reporter Michael McCann calls federal legislation “doomed from the start.” Law scholars Sam Ehrlich and Ryan Rodenberg write that the oversight granted to the NCAA with an anti-trust excemptioon is probably unconstitutional.
Uncertainty around the NCAA has created an enforcement void. Reporting by Ross Dellenger noted that the House settlement has not been effective rule-making due to nonexistant penalties. Conferences are ready to step in, University of Georgia president Jere Morehead, a former chair of the NCAA DI Board of Directors, told Dellenger. “We’d be able to make a much stronger argument that we are not in violation of antitrust rules because we don’t have market power.”
An example of enforcement on a smaller conference-level scale is the 2023 Jim Harbaugh suspension for sign-stealing during a football championship season which preceded NCAA penalties. Rodenberg, in another legal journal article, writes that conferences have an anti-competitive blank check if they’re willing to use it, citing how the Ivy League unilaterly refused scholarships to athletes.
The timing for all of this discussion around college football’s biggest game is no coincidence. The dollars coming to college football winners (schools, conferences, the NCAA and broadcasters) are substantial. The major federal legislation to intervene in college sports has been consistent with a money grab. Student-athlete interests, outside of the small number of big-money sports (football, basketball), factor little into the college sports money grab.
Except, maybe not. Athlete rights figured prominently in the discussion at the Congressional College Athletics Summit organized by Massachusetts Representative Lori Trahan. Trahan, a former college volleyball player, sponsored the latest example of college sports intervention legislation, the College Athletics Reform Act. At the Summit, anti-trust attorney Julia Duncan made the point that college athletes have no seat at the bargaining table, something that will not change much if the NCAA and its member schools gain an anti-trust excemption.
These are the early days of sport policy in the U.S., which is to say the public policy around sport. Australia is a country that has had government sport policy for 50 years, going back to the nation’s dismal performance in the Montreal Olympics, according to a research paper by University of Canberra professor Charles Mountifield.
Mountifield makes the point that where sport policy had at first been a national sports participation initiative, it has lately become a professional athlete and sports media policy. Also of late, Australia is a mostly sedentary nation, like the U.S.
The emerging U.S. sport policy looks like a mistake that underrepresents nearly all college student-athletes, and instead represents university commercial interests that are often not in the best interests of student-athletes.
Awhile back I had a conversation with an analyst who worked for the Chicago Cubs. The injury crisis for baseball pitchers was something he worked on directly, overseeing data sharing between the Cubs, the Tampa Bay Rays and a handful of other MLB teams. Those teams hoped that their data collaboration would help the teams’ researchers to solve, or at least improve, the pitching injuries situation. He also told me about a proposal he had made to MLB to fund biomechanics pitching labs for any NCAA school that would be willing to share its biomechanics and injury-related data. (The idea went nowhere.) If one agrees that data sharing is crucial for reducing sports injuries, then U.S. sport policy can reasonably be understood as, at least in part, public health policy.
A Cubs-like subsidy (sourced to pro sports leagues or to government outlays) could realistically be extended beyond schools with baseball pitchers to any college athlete willing to share their injury-related data, which would be all of their health and performance data. The arrangement would necessitate applied privacy technology (like I work on) to preserve some (though not all) athlete anonymity. At the end of the day the most elite, most valuable athletes would have the most valuable data.
Athletes could be paid according to their exceptionalism within a framework that works for quarterbacks and for water polo players, for SEC athletes and HBCU athletes and Division III athletes, for men and for women. Any organization seeking financial gain from sport policy related to college student-athletes should expect to offset their benefit in ways that support public health efforts around athletes and injuries.


News

Will Stein has a plan to fix Kentucky’s biggest roster red flag from Wildcat Blue Nation blog by Drew Holbrook on January 19
It’s Time to Drop Total Running as a KPI in Football from Hudl blog by Mauro Mandorino on January 15
A new CEMMT blog with Damian Keter summarizes our mega-mechanisms paper sponsored by ForceNET and published in PLOS One. from X/Twitter by Chad Cook on January 13
Is it fair that a reliever gets the win after one inning while a starter throws seven scoreless and gets nothing? This OR/MS Today article proposes a better way to assign credit and fix MLB’s flawed win–loss system from X/Twitter by INFORMS on January 20
Under Armour says it’s ‘aware’ of data breach claims after 72M customer records were posted online from TechCrunch by Zack Whittaker on January 22

title: ADCN: Demand and Capacity, College Sports, Youth Burnout … and More News
text:
Work on developing this newsletter continues. I’m pushing in the direction of something quicker and newsier for 2026. Thanks for reading!

Demand and Capacity
Structural engineers use Demand-Capacity models to understand risk related to buildings and bridges. Capacity refers to the unit’s strength based on its design and material construction. The external forces that the unit experiences is the Demand. When Capacity is greater than Demand we are safe. Athletes’ Injury Risk models based on Training Load data fail to parse Capacity and Demand, neglecting some really useful descriptive details in the process.
Let’s look first at Capacity. Capacity closely approximates Training Load as an indicator of athletes’ fitness. A more complete story for Capacity should also include Readiness parameters that come from recovery, stress and sleep biometrics. To go on with the building analogy, Capacity is the design, construction and ongoing maintenance for athletes. Training, injury and return-to-play from injury are Capacity supplements and reductions.
Examples: Greek researchers showed that even short flights (< 5 hours) deteriorate athletic performance, probably because of lost sleep. Athlete performance consultant Peter Tierney recently posted the benefit of sprint training for athletes, with greater benefit to athletes with high training loads. Whoop posted Christiano Ronaldo’s year-end data summary from the company’s wearable sensing platform.
Demand refers to all of the external forces that apply in the context of athletic competition. Some of these forces are controllable. Some are not. Changes to a game model or game plan can ask more or ask less of specific players. Weather and play surface can be controlled indoors but are subject to unpredictable factors (temperature, traction) outdoors. A team’s or an athlete’s personal goals for individual performance, recognition or achievement can also apply a psychosocial demand.
Examples: The British Journal of Sports Medicine published a study led by Tom Allen from Arsenal that calculated changes in running load and fixture congestion for Premier League players since the 2015-16 season. Allen and co-authors cite, “Running load demands (TD, HID, HSR, SprD, HID/min, HSR/min and SprD/min), total match minutes and congested minutes have increased over the last decade.” Professional soccer athletes James Milner and Steph Houghton also collaborated on an essay for BJSM that documents their experience with more running and more games compared to earlier in their careers.
Demand-Capacity models are, in many cases, instantiated by formulating the Demand-Capacity ratio (DCR). When Demand/Capacity (Demand divided by Capacity) is greater than 1 then it equates to failure in structural engineering situations. Given the relatively rare instances of athletes’ injury, it would be difficult for DCR to predict injury right off the bat so “Injury = Failure” may not be the proper context for an Athletes DCR. A more realistic starting point is to use changes in DCR to describe an athlete’s performance decline in terms of expected or actual values, whether from age or injury or both.
It also makes sense to sum the Demand and the Capacity for all of the individual players on a team in order to generate a team-wide DCR. Injury cascades (and other major Capacity changes) and fixture congestion results (and other major Demand changes) would surface with real clarity.
Teams should also be able to observe positive and negative effects from collaboration and teamwork as teams that work together for easier scores would show lower Demand and lower DCR for the same results. Reports about the recent success of Vanderbilt mens basketball, and its emphasis on unselfish playing style, show the substantial benefit from high levels of players’ cohesion in team sports. At least that is the hypothesis, that better teamwork can reduce individual athletes’ wear and tear.


College Sports, Sport by Sport Changes
I went to Northwestern with ESPN’s college sports writer Dan Murphy who profiled Cody Campbell, describing Campbell’s role in evolving the business models so that U.S. college sports can be a long-term sustainable enterprise. Campbell has also played a major part (as chief financial underwriter) for the football team’s success at Texas Tech. The fix for college sports, according to Campbell, is a change to federal law, one that would enable American universities to collectively negotiate their television contracts, something that would increase (potentially dramatically) revenue and return the money to levels where revenue sports (football, basketball) can support non-revenue sports (everything that’s not football, basketball).
Universities are not waiting for government intervention to decide their options. The financial and competitive pressure has compelled action, and what has occurred so far is on a sport-by-sport basis.
College hockey has changed noticeably since former Canadian Hockey League (CHL) minor league junior hockey players started joining college teams. High-impact CHL freshman are producing for Maine and Penn State. In general, the deeper talent pool has more good teams and more goal-scoring.
College soccer has moved in the direction of a competitive season with games during both the traditional Fall and Spring academic calendars. According to reporting by Sportico, the move syncs with MLS calendar changes and sets up lots of attention for competitive American soccer in April. The new college soccer schedule is more likely to come to NCAA Mens programs before the NCAA Womens programs.
College swimming looks like it will have a 12-team “College Swimming League” with made-for television dual meets that also count as NCAA competitions.
College basketball recently saw an NBA-drafted player, James Nnaji, gain eligibility to play for Baylor. Nnaji might join the team as soon as this January. There may be more college basketball roster spots available for G-League players and NBA draftees looking for a place to earn a better paycheck. (The NCAA has announced rules clarifications saying that drafted basketball players are ineligible.)
A note about college athletes and their data privacy Privacy is barely discussed in the conversations about new business models for college sports. As an athletes’ data privacy researcher who has worked in media businesses I can see how privacy is lower priority than business and financial fundamentals. Data privacy is, however, a central aspect of athletes’ basic rights. When discussions turn to asking what’s best for athletes (as opposed to university or business interests) the subject of privacy emerges. In this situation privacy has become a bellwether for identifying (or ignoring) athletes’ interests in discussions about the changes ahead for college sports.


Youth Sports Burnout
A feature-length article by Ken Belson looks at how and why large numbers of child athletes are failing to thrive within their youth sport experiences. Specialization, competition rank high on a long list of anxiety-inducing pressures that coaches, parents and organized youth sports in general combine to inflict on pre-teens and teens. 70 percent of child athletes drop out by age 13. Belson writes that viewing the problems in youth sports through a mental health lens is a path forward. Coping is a mechanism for kids to possibly find the fun that previous generations did not have to look so hard to find in sports.
With the 2026 Winter Olympics close to its start, a Wisconsin newspaper asked famous Olympic speed skater Eric Heiden for thoughts about the promising 21-year old U.S. speed skater Jordan Stolz. Heiden is legendary for winning short- and long-distance races, something that young skaters no longer have an opportunity to pursue. Training programs have become specialized for young skaters. Heiden feels that there is a mental advantage that comes from taking on challenges that don’t materialize if it is a singular focus on winning.
Stolz made it through the gauntlet and is on the verge of his Olympic dreams. Most child athletes won’t realize Olympics or professional dreams, which should be obvious. But if two-thirds of athletes don’t reach higher levels of competition then the athlete development pipeline reduces dramatically. Specialization, it seems, has a lot to do with why. “Exceptional young performers reached their peak quickly but narrowly mastered only one interest (e.g., one sport). By contrast, exceptional adults reached peak performance gradually with broader, multidisciplinary practice. However, elite programs are designed to nurture younger talent,” is the finding from a review article published this week in Science.
Eric Cressey is Director of Player Performance for the New York Yankees and a long-time personal trainer for young baseball talents. As described in an essay from earlier this year Cressey saw a unique training window in his young daughter’s rehabilitation for a broken arm. “It’s on us as coaches to a) identify where the massive growth potential exists, b) make the athlete/parents/coach aware of it, and c) close the gap with good programming and quality coaching,” writes Cressey, making points that are at odds with the benefit from increased autonomy mentioned in the Belson article. These windows of “adaption,” according to Cressey, “set the state for more long-term success.” But if these windows come with the dependency on coaches to recognize and program then burnout should surprise no one.


More News

Perspectives on sports analytics to inform constraint manipulation, representative learning and functional variability in practice design Human Movement Science journal by Ben Teune et al. on Dec 21
The National Science Foundation just had a big reorganization. Here are five things to know Science, ScienceInsider by Jeffrey Mervis on Dec 29

Peripheral Labs taps into self-driving car sensors to bring sports fans right into the game TechCrunch by Ivan Mehta on Dec 18

title: ADCN: Public Health, Nutrition, Injury Algorithms
text:
As I get the newsletter up and running it might take a few weeks to work out a regular production schedule. My goal is to have a regular once per week delivery by Thanksgiving, and then ramp up to twice per week by year end. The amount of available material is substantial, and that is exciting. Eventually, and probably soon, I should have a succinct description for what this newsletter covers and why. The voice will be technically dense, culturally relevant and fun to read. There will also be a website spinning up at https://newsletter.bradstenger.com as the newsletter comes together. I am using the Quarto publishing system and I am excited about the possibilities that the platform offers.

Sports, Infectious Disease and Public Health Policy
a smaller CDC puts athletes at risk
The number of compromised athletes from illness far exceeded the number of compromised athletes from injury at the most recent Swimming World Championships in Singapore. The cause was a contagious gastroenteritis that came with U.S. swimmers from their training camp in Thailand. Elite athletes normally use 5-star facilities, and before COVID they could normally count on an environment that would not make them sick. COVID changed the rules, and no place, no matter the expense, could assure total confidence.
Confidence in public health is about to get worse as the U.S. Centers for Disease Control (CDC) shrinks in size and responsibility. Leagues, teams and athletes will probably want to anticipate worst case scenarios, COVID-level precautions, in order to assure athlete health when there is regional, national or international travel for competition, whether that is for games or tournaments or Olympics or World Cups. The potential exposure combined with the high stakes for athletes’ health and performance makes it almost necessary to institute bubble-like preventive measures in order to make it more likely to identify contagious infections sooner and not later. And as public health infrastructure degrades the need increases to step up bubble-type precautions. For example, personal health-monitoring measurements using wearables has been shown to identify infections before symptoms appear.
How bad will public health get? It looks like that depends on where you go. Vermont, where I am, looks like it will establish public health monitoring in an alliance with the other New England states. California, Oregon and Washington plan something similar on the West coast. Properly-funded government public infrastructure has an important role in developing monitoring technologies. The wastewater monitoring that helped to corral COVID is set to improve as AI and functional genomics describe real-time risk inherent to changes that occur with mutating pathogenic vectors. Eventually the tech will make it possible to where and why infectious disease risk is high. But that’s not the world we have right now.


Personalized Nutrition
nutrition is an n=1 experiment
More than 80% of student-athletes at the University of Georgia eat at Chipotle at least once per week. The figure is 98% for Ohio State student-athletes. The statistics are part of the announcement that Chipotle now gives student-athletes at Georgia, Ohio State and University of Florida (> 90%) coupons for free food during their sports seasons.
There’s a trend among food product developers to target demand among active populations with new offerings. Supplements like creatine, protein and collagen have shown increased effectiveness in active populations where inactive populations do not benefit, coming from Mike Ormsbee in his conversation with podcaster (and restauranteur) David Chang. The same should be true for substantial, relatively unprocessed fast casual burrito bowls.
Ultra-endurance athletes have begun to seize competitive advantages from fueling strategies that make sure energy stores are not depleted. The nutrition programs have become personalized and precise. Continued progress to personalized nutrition will accelerate as the research continues. There’s a good chance that future will look something like Chipotle where my order (burrito: 2x chicken, pinto beans, cheese and lettuce) is what works for me and not necessarily anyone else. The minimally processed, substantial mix of quality proteins and carbohydrates hits at the lowest common denominator of what an athlete needs but there is no single recipe that works for all athletes.
The question of how to eat becomes more important than what to eat if it becomes incumbent for each athlete to be their own personalized nutrition expert. A recent Stanford study shows that blood sugar responses to carbohydrate intake is highly variable, and depends on individual health and metabolism. But where subtypes for general pre-diabetic populations can be measured, each and every high-performance athlete might be their own singular subtype.


Predicting Athletes’ (Re)Injuries
expect regular info on how algorithms and models for injury continue to evolve
New research out of Australia took data from one Australian Football League club to model athletes’ re-injury susceptability. The study takes athlete data for 1 season and 1 team, combining injury data for 33 athletes with performance tracking data 2500+ training and competition sessions. The injury prediction model comes from ~100 injuries, meaning that very few sessions had injuries occur, pointing to the obvious benefit from data sharing among teams in order to base results on data with more injuries and more data sets.
The work combines survival modeling and a Hidden Markov Model, an approach that tracks a range of known covariates and also incorporates hidden, unknown or unknowable factors but still provides measurable reliability for predictions. Age, it turns out, is the leading covariate factor affecting re-injury. But "factors relating to the number of efforts exceeding 95%ile in terms of speed, perceived exertion and acceleration had significant affects on risk of re-injury."
The results are somewhat consistent with a 5000-person study of injured runners that gained attention this summer. Danish researchers used Garmin data for recreational runners and identified 1820 injured athletes. Large spikes in training distances were the most frequent cause of overuse injuries which authors found to occur in 30% of runners who had at least 200 running sessions.
In both the elite athlete who is recovering from injury, or the recreational athlete who trains irregularly, it is essential to prepare for maximum effort endeavors.

title: ADCN: Team Building, Research Funding & Methods, News
text:
Hope 2026 is off to a great start! This newsletter project is rounding into shape behind the scenes. I’m ready to get your feedback and incorporate any notes you have for ADCN scope and content. Just reply to this email and I should get your note directly. Thanks!

The Science and The Art of Team Building
Kent Hughes likes his team. He’s the general manager of the Montreal Canadiens, and he told a recent media gaggle that his team’s recent success was “what happens when a team plays for each other.” As evidence, he pointed to a recent article in The Athletic by Rustin Dodd, that asked “How do you hold a star athlete accountable?”
Coaches have their approaches for player accountability, and results are mixed. Honest confrontational approaches fail as often as they succeed. One example cited by Dodd, a star-coach disagreement involving Memphis Grizzlies’ Ja Morant and coach Tuomas Iisalo where Iisalo called out Morant in front of the team a few weeks ago. Since then Memphis’ season is floundering, five games below .500. Morant looks like he will soon be traded.
The more successful approach mentioned in the article is team accountability. “The most effective mode of accountability was not handed down by a manager but instead derived from the emotional connections that employees had for each other,” according to Cornell professor Diedre Snyder. (This is her paper.) “It’s teams that know each other, trust each other, have an emotional connection to each other.” Leaders matters but only to create a culture where players prioritize not letting their teammates down.
Dodd has another recent article that put coaching leadership under a microscope, specifically the leadership style of New England Patriots head coach Mike Vrabel. Vrabel is an example of a coach who pushes his players outside their comfort zones in an effort to achieve superior performance. Again, the idea is based on science. Paul Zak is a cognitive neuroscience researcher and an expert in leadership, and he feels that effective coaching requires a certain amount of bullying.
“Our brains are so energy hungry,” Zak tells Dodd. “They’re just such an enormous energy (drain) in the body that we have evolved to kind of cruise most of the time. It’s like, ‘I really don’t want to expend the metabolic energy of doing something cognitively or physically difficult unless I’m really pushed to do that.’ So we need to be pushed. And I’m going to celebrate it when you reach these goals.”
Zak was among the first brain scientists to recognize the significance of oxytocin, a hormone that contributes to feelings of attachment, closeness and trust. Zak also acknowledges that the forces which compel human interaction like appearances and behaviors play critical roles alongside neurobiology. An organization can create settings for neurological bonding but lack the necessary person-to-person interaction, and vice versa. It takes the right mix of personalities throughout a coaching staff and locker room to get culture right.
When we interview student-athletes as part of my research we probe their coach-athlete interactions and we ask every question we can think of when it comes to choosing between personal development and team collaboration. What’s most interesting about their answers is how their young adult thoughts come at a time when their judgements begin to form. The fact that we get thoughtful answers to these questions is, I think, indicative that the push-pull between self-development and team-contribution is an essential aspect of team sports participation. Just like every athlete differs in their physical capabilities, their mental and cognitive approach is also uniquely their own.
Are you building a team, or a collection of talent? The answer has to be both. The big difference: A collection of talent makes sense on a spreadsheet while a team makes sense in the personal interactions of everyone involved.


Sports Research Funding, Sports Research Methods
David Spergel oversees science grantmaking at the Simons Foundation and he spoke with a magazine editor about the shifts in US science policy. Research and research funding are intimately connected. Spergel sees two possible futures for science funding in America, “One is that this proposed budget is a bump in the road and funding will stay at close to the same level. A second version is where this represents a real sea change in the relationship between government and science funding. I don’t think we know what world we’re in yet.”
Universities have responded to their new reality and have cut back on new hires. Don’t expect to see big new program announcements for sports research at American universities. The last two announcements that I saw come from University of Michigan and University of California-Berkeley. Michigan launched a $25 million research partnership with ASICS, a Japanese shoe and apparel company. Berkeley created the Women’s Health and Performance Initiative led by the school’s Institute for Security and Governance with external partner SPD Sports and internal partners in Athletics, Public Health and Data Science.
The agreements give two Asian sports manufacturers access to expertise, lab facilities, teams and athletes. I am not familiar with changing norms in Asian participatory sports but the 2032 Summer Olympics will be in Australia, giving Asian athletes a proximity advantage and increased public attention. The partnership may also signal new (or perhaps improved) recruitment pipelines to elite Asian athletes into the schools’ Olympics and non-Olympics sports.
Access to teams has become every bit as important as access to athletes for sports research. The number of research articles in the subject area of “athletic performance” which mention “field-based” methods jumped to 46 for the years 2024 and 2025 from 10 in years 2022 and 2023. Field-based methods are studies which collect data in athletes’ playing and practicing environment, and not in a laboratory (or other tightly controlled setting).
Stellingwerff et al. published a review that is also a how-to for conducting field-based research during training camps and in competition. The authors acknowledge that they sacrifice experimental control and accuracy by taking their study outside the lab, but “development of more portable measurement equipment (e.g., indirect calorimetry, desktop blood/gas analyzers, portable laboratories, etc.) and/or wearable or consumable technologies (e.g., smart watches, sensors, core temp pills, etc.)” make field-based study more compelling.
The University of Michigan started the Athlete Innovation Initiative in the Human Performance & Sport Science Center in 2024. This program trains student researchers in new athletic data collection technologies, and connects their expertise to Michigan student-athletes and teams. Before this year of research cutbacks, there had been momentum at major US sports universities to connect relevant research labs to sports teams, including field-based investigations.
Two Michigan middle-distance track athletes, Miles Brown and Ian Hill, who both plan to go to medical school describe their experience in a university news article. “A lot of what we were doing was looking at the different sports at the university, especially the sports that would be interested in doing some testing in this lab, and identifying what tests we could do that could be predictive of performance or injury prevention or whatever that sport would want,” said Ian Hill.
With change comes uncertainty. Spergel makes the point that there is no way for philanthropy to come close to filling government’s role in funding research. If the government-university research funding relationship is permanently changed, Spergel expects universities to identify and focus on strengths. That assumption fits with all of the changes to the American college sports business model where universities will have to make business decisions that will affect student-athletes.
My research funding from National Science Foundation expires at the end of the year. I hope that there is a path forward for research like mine, on privacy and athletes’ data. I’m not exactly sure where to look for it. The student-athletes we talk to generally approve of sharing data for research but it always exists in the context of a player-team dynamic. There’s opportunity for expanding field-based research and for connecting it to lab-based work.
Help Me to Follow the Money   I’ll do my best to report the announcements for sports research initiatives that occur in North America. If you hear things, please let me know. Thanks!

title: ADCN: Health Algorithms & Interfaces, Data Duty of Care, Injury Reports
text:
The ADCN newsletter archive can be found at https://newsletter.bradstenger.com. I mention it here for two reasons. First, the site construction needs some attention and some work, and this public announcement will make it more likely to see those improvements occur sooner rather than later. Second, the website has a sign up where anyone can give their email to join ADCN’s mailing list. I’m still soft-launching ADCN but the public launch is coming. Getting the ADCN web presence up and running is a crucial step.

Health Algorithms and the Lowest Common Denominator
Personalization algorithms, in general, do not work so well when those algorithms have to serve thousands (or millions) of users. The situation is a classic case where usability has to function at a lowest common denominator across a population where the variation is substantial.
For some people, especially people who trust machine instructions with little or no question, and in terms of demographics this population skews young, there is also an inabilitiy to contextualize recommendations that come from an AI source. Failing to understand AI in context makes human error likely despite confidence that is out of proportion with reality, mostly because reality gets ignored.
Every once in a while the scientific literature presents a window into health data recommendation systems and its lowest common denominator. Apple recently published in PLOS One results from the company’s sleep tracking study that took data from more than 35,000 Apple watch wearers who use the device as a sleep tracker. Most sleep data collection occurs in 1-14 day windows, predominantly at the low end, and it under-estimates the night to night sleep variability across populations. Apple simulated longer duration datasets and subsampled that data in an attempt to understand the depth of the problem with short-duration sleep samples.
Apple researchers stratified groups into one-hour intervals, and came to realize that the smaller group of lighter sleepers (6 +/- 1 hours) could not be differentiated from the more common heavier (7 +/- 1 hours) sleepers. “For the planning of future [sleep measurement] studies, there may be a resource and/or participant burden tension between sample size and duration of tracking,” the author conclude, saying “the path to mitigation lies in duration of tracking per participant, more so than boosting cohort sample size.”
Sleep is incredibly complex. Recent mouse studies suggest close links between seasonal variation in diet and circadian rhythm. Circadian rhythm may need to be measured for long (probably very long) duration in order to understand seasonal, annual, reproductive, and human life stage variations in sleep, and in turn, recovery and performance. These are evolutionary forces at work where subtle and deep-seated adaptions have major, meaningful implications. Human physiological and cognitive adaptions for running and cooking helped to shape human existence over long term time frames. Is socialized, high exertion, inter-personal competition, also known as sports, fundamentally human and a generator of physiological and cognitive adaption? I wouldn’t rule it out.
So what is AI good for when it comes to health and performance data? Arjun Mahajan and Stephen Gilbert gave their opinion in a Nature Digital Medicine essay, “Do we need AI guardians to protect us from health information overload?” Guardians are necessary, they write, because commercial health data devices send too many low-value alerts that miss what a health assistant should target, which are “filtering, contextualizing, and personalizing health information” for users.
Threshold values in biometric data are a consistently useful interaction that the authors mention, and they do best when the data user specifies the target threshold (like heart rate during zone training). User-specified targets are personalized, contextualized, and filtered, all because a human sets all of those parameters when they go about creating the target alert on their device. Keeping a human in the feedback seems far more useful than a completely automated AI when it comes to data-backed health and performance information.
Sports have coaches. If path to personalization benefits from human input then there should be a place for coaches’ input on behalf of athletes, especially when it comes to determining useful athletes’ thresholds. Putting a coach (or coaches) in the feedback loop should help to avoid low-value alerts and to keep things personal, contextual and filtered. Problems will arise when misaligned incentives prompt coaches to not prioritize athletes’ best interests. In general athletes trust coaches. Give coaches the benefit of the doubt and to make their input for athlete threshold settings a default (or a low-effort optional setting), at least to start.
How things start might not be how things end up. There are signs that AI recommendation systems can and will be gamed by a servcice’s self-interests, and not in users’ best interests. A post at Bruce Schneier’s blog describes the phenomena of AI Summary Optimization. The situation is analogous to Search Engine Optimization from 10-15 years ago. AISO is the new SEO. Keywords and content sequences, often jargon, factor into the subjective assessment made by an AI recommender system. Start down the path from automated threshold notification to subjective recommendations and from there it is a slippery slope to low-value alerts.


Duty of Care for Data
The FAIR consensus reports are a series of review papers that examine “prevention strategies and modifiable risk factors” for female, woman and girl athletes. The papers have been appearing regularly in the British Journal of Sports Medicine and cover their review process and the targets for injury prevention such as concussion, upper extremities, lower extremities, etc. The series recently concluded with articles and essays that discuss dissemination and implementation of FAIR findings and recommendations. The challenge of translating medicine and transferring technology from research to practice is difficult on its easiest day. Any how-to guide will still have difficulty leading interested stakeholders to successful outcomes. Competence and its polar opposite, negligence, are difficult to identify during research translation, and without identifiable best and worst practices, the road map to a better place (or away from a worse place) is not clear or obvious.
If there is one guiding principal for athletes’ health it is the idea of duty of care. Duty of care is a legal standard and a social norm and, in general, reflects an individual’s responsibility for the well-being of others. Duty of care means that coaches do not overwork players, that one player does not cheap shot another player, that equipment is safe, that medical staff are available, and any other measure to prevent forseeable harm. Duty of care is also a legal standard, and when breached can result in judgements of negligence and liability.
Duty of care does not as yet extend to athletes’ data and information, either in its legal or social context. But maybe it should. Duty of care already exists in the context of injury prevention. Greater awareness of its potential application to athletes’ data and information would incentivize action to translate research into practice.
Another recent BJSM review paper is an audit of the effect female menstrual cycles have on sports injuries of all types and among all genders. (This was not a FAIR consensus review.) Injury analysis papers, the authors found, look primarily at evidence from male athletes and when they do consider female athletes’ evidence it does not account for hormonal fluctuations, despite the significant bearing that menstrual cycles have on injury occurrence. There’s a case to make that any aggregate injury analysis needs to account for cycles and their role. There’s also a case to make that cycles have enormous variation and it will take great effort to understand and account for the role of menstrual cycles in sports injury. Duty of care would suggest that there is an essential need to understand injury occurrences and prevention. If some analysis that takes shortcuts can help, those studies are valuable. But there is also an obligation to do the difficult, time-consuming work to understand the full scope of athlete injuries.
Concussions, among all athletes, have long been a duty of care quagmire. Recall how long it took for American football to recognize its problem with concussions. Looking the other way was far easier than solving the difficult problem of football concussions when solutions meant changing fundamentals of the game, but eventually duty of care was applied and change happened. Concussion diagnosis still does not have objective criteria and relies on athletes’ self-reporting (often reluctant). Canada’s Sport Information Resource Centre took the stance that concussion symptoms reporting is a shared “duty of care” responsibility that cuts across all sports’ participants and at every level. Sports participation should not exist within a broader sports culture “that tolerates (and sometimes celebrates) pain and injury, and privileges performance over health,” the authors write. The authors are sport injury researchers at Canadian universities and they emphasize awareness and communication in their “it takes a village” solution.
A recent meeting among Michigan high school sports administrators pointed to the considerable costs, inconsistent practices, and high stakes, the greatest of which are to athletes who are false-positives and pass their on-field cognitive evalutation. The need is for fast, accurate concussion exams in a Canada-like framework that prioritizes long-term health and well-being.
The legal dimension to duty of care makes it intimidating to cite duty of care in any context where there is risk of litigation. Personal injury law can be seen working at the periphery of sports to call out negligence and liability. The more important context is athletes’ health and well-being however, and duty of care should motivate the advances in sports management and coaching practice that research and technology make possible.


Injury Reports
Haralabob Voulgaris made tens of millions of dollars as a professional gambler on sports. Speaking with Pablo Torre on a podcast Voulgaris said that the NBA has a real integrity problem when it came to the team injury reports, especially with regard to the relationship between those reports and gambling. Voulgaris did not cite any specific example of improper or illegal activity but did describe how different teams approach the injury reports differently, with varying levels of effort and accuracy. Injury report information is crucial to serious sports gamblers who are anxious to know anything that could favor (or disfavor) their action. The team-by-team differences in report production and quality make it difficult to police public information which make it possible for insider information to leak (or spill) out in sports gambling contexts.
The news about injury reports is not really about the NBA though. The NCAA now requires player availability reports for the 2026 men’s and women’s Division 1 basketball tournaments. Public injury reports have been required for football in the SEC, Big Ten, ACC and Mid-American conferences. The SEC and ACC also require reports for men’s and women’s basketball, and for baseball. The NCAA pointed out that the reports keep gambling interests from interacting directly with student-athletes about their health status.
A medical school student and two professors wrote an opinion essay in the medical news outlet STAT, saying that the personal information contained in team injury reports would constitute a serious privacy violation if they had not been subject to collective bargaining and contractual language between the athletes and their leagues. The origin of injury reports dates to 1947 when the NFL commission learned of dramatic shifts in betting leading up to a game where a flu had significantly compromised one team. The authors, two Yale doctors and a Case Western lawyer, say that it’s enough to say that an athlete will not compete, and any potentially invasive details are not necessary. As NCAA athletes become more subject to injury reporting requirements there is no indication who or how anyone will defend their privacy interests in the disclosures.

title: ADCN: Baseball Player Development, Soccer Data Sharing, News
text:
Please help me to grow this newsletter and forward it to anyone you think might be interested. Anyone can sign up for the mailing list via the form at newsletter.bradstenger.com. Thanks!

Athlete Development: Baseball and Every Other Sport
Prospects alert! It’s almost time to begin playing professional baseball. Advances in baseball player development have made college and pro baseball compelling in a way that’s far different from five or ten years ago. The Moneyball foundation of homeruns and strikeouts still defines the modern baseball product and is still monotonous. The good pitchers keep getting injured, which is a problem. But new faces and the dramatic improvement in player skill and performance are eye-opening. It’s an innovation story and who doesn’t like an innovation story.



Josh Hejka answers a question at the Wake Forest baseball seminar in December, 2025.


Josh Hejka made the point during a panel at the 2025 Wake Forest Player Development Seminar that the majority of MLB baseball players have reached that level on talent alone. Their development trajectory is just beginning and some percentage will flourish with access to elite biomechanics training programs. So this might be the best part of the season, when you get to see bold! predictions on this year’s new faces in MLB. You can read Eno Sarris’ giant report on Stuff+ pitcher rankings (which also includes a functional health and sustainability assessment, meant for fantasy baseball but super-interesting nonetheless).
The innovation story around athlete development is big in baseball, but it hits every sport. You can see it in football and quarterback development is central to this year’s Super Bowl. The path to the big game has been different for the Patriots’ Drake Maye and the Seahawks’ Sam Darnold. This article in The Ringer describes how each teams’ offensive coordinator helped their quarterback’s decision-making. There are two ways for a quarterback to read the situation during a passing play.
A quarterback can pre-process the situation if he knows the limitations of the defensive alignment. But football teams have gotten better at disguising defenses and an alternative method for quarterbacking emerged. The quarterback should instead progress from one option, Plan A, to the next, Plan B, and so on. A progression is an easier cognitive problem for a quarterback and with good play design produces winning results. It’s still possible though for smart operators at quarterback and at game planning to get their desired results through preprocessing. The Seahawks are the progression team. The Patriots are the preprocessing team. One team will win Super Bowl LX.
I plan to tell more innovation and player development stories for basketball, hockey and soccer, and for college sports in the ADCN. Athlete development is, in many ways, the demand side for athletes’ health and performance data. The supply side is not just the athletes. There are also the technologies to collect and manage the athletes’ data that inform player development. Some of my research into privacy and athletes’ data is precautionary. If I do it well, some of my the work will head off problems that might otherwise slow down the innovation around athlete development. I will be telling those stories in the ADCN too.


Data Sharing in Sports: Some of the How and Why
The soccer data site FBref no longer has access to the a rich data pipeline provided to it by Opta and StatsPerform. FBref had been considered to be the most comprehensive no-cost public football data resource before the change. The loss creates a void in the soccer analytics community and a major drop in the data resources for global women’s soccer.
This comes at a time when the NFL’s 2026 Big Data Bowl nears its conclusion. Michael Lopez, the Senior Director of Football Data and Analytics, told the Wharton Moneyball podcast that data is a football Field of Dreams, “If you have the data and make it available, they will analyze it.” Data availability is an opportunity for innovation and for community. According to Lopez, the competition and the data availability has strong support from the league and its member teams. The Big Data Bowl also benefits from a complimentary data set that is robust and no cost at nflfastr (accessible using R and Python).
Major League Baseball has its Statcast pitch-by-pitch data service. This incredible resource made it possible for Connor Moore to use machine learning and biomechanics to reverse engineer pitcher health from the velocity, location and pitch type of their throws. The work earned Moore his PhD at Clemson University.
I used to manage the APIs at The New York Times and we had strong support for making its content data free and accessible. These were tools that the company’s developers could use to help create new products. More important was the participation that the free API generated in the college student hackathon community. The cost was small compared to the benefit. The same appears to be true for the NFL.
Opta and Stats Perform have data services as their main business. (I interned at Stats during grad school.) A data business may not have the public mission of a prestigous news organization or a sports league, and probably needs to enlist a benefactor in order to provide data services that will enable free distribution. There is a decent chance that at some point FIFA or another global soccer operator will restore some kind free data access.
Or it won’t. There does not appear to be any public component to NBA player tracking or biomechanics data. NHL shot and lineup data is available at MoneyPuck but the advanced player tracking data the the NHL uses for its Edge stats and information site. Corey Sznajder maintains a hockey microstats data service at allthreezones.com, something that invites comparison to Baseball Savant and Statcast, which started as a personal data project before MLB took it over.
Sharing data and making money, business makes it so the two go together, but sometimes it’s not an easy fit. Innovations in technology, in computing, in human performance create opportunities for new business models. New ways to do both, share data and make money, will surface as athletes’ data supply and demand continue to evolve. No doubt it is a big help for innovators and entrepreneurs to have access to quality sports data resources at no cost.

title: ADCN: No-Moneyball, Hockeyification, Injury Surveillance, News
text:
Bill Carter, a UVM professor and authority on student-athletes and the business of college sports, founded NIL Forum, a membership community that’s everything you need to stay ahead in NIL. The community is a needed resource. If you are a stakeholder in U.S. college sports, which is everyone who works in U.S. sports, you should join at the founding member price of $300 for the year.

No-Moneyball: An Innovation Narrative
The most interesting story in sports, in my opinion, is the Colorado Rockies.

I have been asking for opinions about why there is not more data sharing in professional sports. My assumption going into the investigation had been that a team’s desire to have or maintain competitive advantage was the number one reason for teams’ not sharing data. The opposite appears to be true. Teams don’t risk losing their own competitive advantage as much as they risk a smarter, better-resourced team increasing their competitive advantage with the benefit of shared data.
The original Oakland A’s moneyball showed that ingenuity could supplant financial resources. The new situation shows that ingenuity cannot supplant smart money. This is no-moneyball. It’s not that teams cannot have an innovation advantage, but financial resources are a force multiplier that gives richer teams more opportunity to exploit their good ideas and then make those good ideas into even better follow-ups.
An ESPN article looks at Paul DePodesta becoming the president of baseball operations for Colorado Rockies baseball. What’s interesting is how he plans to uncover and to eventually exploit the unique advantages of baseball in Denver. Baseball in Denver is completely different from baseball in other metros, and not in ways that help the home team.
The altitude differences favor hitters over pitchers. The fitness advantage that soccer, hockey, and basketball teams have at altitude goes away with baseball’s deliberate pace. DePodesta has, in many ways, a blank slate to formulate a new calculus for winning baseball by uncovering advantages that are unique to Colorado. It will have to be a different kind of baseball that wins in Denver.
The Rockies situation is consistent with the trend toward constraints-led approaches in athlete training, but with constraints that are severe, affect everyone, and, in many cases, are still to be determined. This is where analysis needs invention in order to get some kind of foothold. Prior Rockies management regimes never found their foothold. Previous management never effectively differentiated players, skills, and game models. The Rockies had no chance to put those differences together into a sum greater than its parts.
The cause and effect between biomechanics and ball tracking have progressed to where the Rockies should be able to understand everything that can and does happen to a mile-high baseball in an MLB game. The article mentions low-spin gyrosliders as a pitch that might work better than traditional breaking balls at Coors Field. The Rockies have been teaching gyroballs to the pitching staff.
The thing that really stacks the odds against Colorado is that any advantage DePodesta finds goes away for half the team’s games. Even if he solves the Rockies’ home-field disadvantage, he also has to generate solutions for the always different, lower-altitude road games. Because the deck is stacked against the Rockies, the list of solutions will need to be long. The quantity of the Rockies’ successful innovations will be the level of the teams’ success.
Innovation stories are stories I can report and write better than most. The writer sets the stakes and chooses the details that matter for readers. Characters matter, but only so far as they contribute to the problem that is at the center of what goes on. As long as readers keep learning, you can expect them to stay with you. The most important thing for the writer, the characters, and the story is capturing the moment when an insight changes everything. That moment and that insight are the payoffs for all the effort by the readers. And the insight lingers, so a reader will probably give the writer (me!) more chances to give them more of this innovation story stuff. That’s how I approach it.
I’m looking forward to the insight and the moment that is building for Paul DePodesta and the Colorado Rockies. There is also a risk that the Rockies’ search for competitive advantage leads to unprecedented surveillance of athletes. Will the Rockies go too far? Maybe. It’s another thing that I am eager to pay attention to.


Information Overload and the Hockeyification Trend
Ben Lindbergh, the Fangraphs podcaster and writer at The Ringer, asks an important question for our data + sports era: how much information is too much information? He asks in the context of professional baseball scouting cards. These are small information sheets with relevant but probably obscure information contained on physical media (paper). An example scouting card would have, say, details for a pitcher or a fielder on how to pitch or how to position for a pinch-hitter.
Sports that have discrete play-by-play events, like baseball and football, lend themselves to this sort of information augmentation. Bill Walsh and the 49ers scripted the first 15 offensive plays of a game. ESPN reported last season that 20 NFL starting quarterbacks use wristband play sheets, something that pre-dates Walsh and goes back to 1965. Football information augmentation has been a collaboration between assistant coaches and players who work together on formulating the shorthand and then producing a super-dense information design that meets on-field needs.
Baseball isn’t quite at that level but could be headed there. Lindbergh points out that assistant coaches have started playing a larger role in the catcher-pitcher collaboration. Some teams have assistant coaches calling pitches instead of catchers. Lindbergh also wishes that players could just play the game without the extra interference, recalling what Crash Davis told Nuke LaLoosh, “Don’t think. It can only hurt the ballclub.”
If there is one sports statistic that associates with overthinking, it would be win probability. Win probability asks a model to project the likelihood of winning between two teams at each game second given the score and situation, even the very first seconds of a game.
A game like baseball has no clock, so win probability, expected runs, and every other advanced stat rise and fall with every pitch. Putting a coach in charge of win probability is a step back from players deciding who wins. Ambitious coaches who want to raise their profile seem glad to have more on their plate. I don’t think that anyone has asked the players how they feel.
Megan Chayka did mention “analytics fatigue” in passing during a hockey analytics panel at the Sloan Sports conference. I think that she meant it in reference to NHL hockey players who are five months into their season.
The cognitive overhead of analysts’ input into how to win games may not necessarily sync with the fast, instinctive hockey that they would otherwise play. The free-flowing action in hockey games makes it difficult for coaches to interject information to the players. Hockey players are asked to hold onto the details without anything close to a scout card or wristband. Analytics fatigue is, I expect, a real thing.
There are calls to make soccer and basketball more free-flowing, like hockey. Soccer teams have increased the tactical importance of set pieces, and according to Mark Thompson writing for his blog, the change has reduced the action during other parts of the game. Baseball has put rules in place to speed the pace of play. Those rules, like pitch clocks, have improved the product.
Call it hockeyification. More action improves any pro sports entertainment product, but more analytics does not lead to the same surefire entertainment upgrade.
The problem is that more action AND more analytics wear down the athletes. Trainers will tell you that stress is stress, something that oversubscribed college athletes regularly experience when their athletics and academics overlap. Universities have learned to provide mental health support for stressed athletes. Teams have an opportunity to get out in front of the potential problems that come with players’ analytics fatigue.


Injury Surveillance
A new report in the Science and Medicine in Football journal by Wilke et al. documents player health surveillance across global soccer’s leagues and tournaments. Most leagues (29/49) do some kind of injury surveillance, while most tournaments (9/14) do not. The researchers conclude that “ongoing programmes are concentrated in a few countries, unevenly distributed across confederations, and higher in men’s competitions compared to women’s, though surveillance has been planned in several women’s leagues.”
Another recent study published in Science and Medicine in Football asked return-to-play practitioners how they differentiated between their expert judgment and data-driven analyses. The authors found a conflict between quantitative evidence and expert opinion regarding whether delaying RTP reduces injury risks. The analyses says delayed RTP does not reduce injury risks, whereas experts insist that delayed RTP does reduce injury risks.
Filling a need that exists between injury surveillance and RTP is a tool for risk management created by the Aspetar sports medicine center in Qatar. The conflict between data and opinion re: athletes’ health needs objective computational tools to deliver guidance that bridges the gap. There are other examples of new computational tools: (1.) The English FA and Google Cloud created the Female Athlete Health Framework - link, (2.) Swedish researchers apply systems immunology to assess athletes’ physical capacity - link, (3.) American researchers make a case for increasing the role for pharmacists in sports medicine - link, (4.) Australian researchers get in front of health challenges for 2026 World Cup in North America - link.
Domain experts are needed to assemble this information in collaboration with computing professionals, and if it is done well, athletes will benefit.


News

title: ADCN: Cycling, Induced Demand, Collaboration, News
text:
If you enjoy reading ADCN and can recommend it to your colleagues please point them to the signup box at newsletter.bradstenger.com. Thanks!

Cycling is the Sports Science Model Activity
Richard Kemp does good work describing new training technologies for competitive cyclists. Endurance cycling was the original domain for Dave Brailsford’s training philosophy of marginal gains. “The core principle was that if you made 1% improvements in multiple areas, you could reap significant rewards overall,” wrote Tim Lewis in The Guardian. Brailsford’s Team GB cyclists dominated the 2008 and 2012 Olympics and the Tour de France during the 2010s.
Lewis’ article, from 2019, makes the point that marginal gains had started to exhibit diminishing returns. Kemp is smart and recognizes that new training technologies might be a marginal gain, or they might be a costly waste of time and energy. This new Kemp article on biosensors in Cycling Weekly follows up another Kemp technology review from October 2025.
Kemp looks at real-time monitors for core body temperature, oxygen use and ventilation, hydration, and blood glucose. My short summary: accuracy is generally lacking, but the delta, the metric’s real-time change in measurement, can be useful. These devices are oversold as real-time indicators since their data plus algorithms often benefit from the additional context that comes later.
Yannis Pitsiladis of Hong Kong Baptist University is an expert that Kemp relies on who says that the technology is “still in its early brick-phone stage.” It works, but “it’s not yet miniaturised or fully integrated. The next step is ecosystem-level sensing - multiple validated inputs communicating in real time.”
Cycling is the model athletic activity, a relatively simple example that generates insight into everything else that is more complex. A human on a bicycle is the most efficient transport mechanism on earth, better than birds, fish, horses, and cars. Power measurements can be captured with every pedal stroke. Each bike rider has their own bike even though they sometimes ride in teams.
Cyclists’ endurance, more than their pure speed or power, is what puts them on podiums. Training decides who wins cycling races, something that is true for all kinds of high-level competition, but more true with bikes. For example, Jonas Vingegaard seems poised for a breakthrough against Tadej Pogacar in this summer’s Tour de France.
As the training data gains precision, the winners become more likely to be predetermined by athletes’ volume and quality of training. Marcus Elliott, the P3 founder, once told me that he doesn’t really like to watch sports. He said that he already knows how a game will unfold because he can see how players move and how fit they all are.
If that assessment sounds like a gambling edge, it is. Athletes’ training data can be similarly revealing. Gambling, and the demand for athletes’ data it generates, is something that will have to be accounted for once these wearable biometrics move beyond the brick-phone stage.


Sports are a Highway
A Bloomberg article on the total cost of urban highways reminded me how traffic economics feels to me like sports injury prevention efforts. Despite enormous effort to improve things, the number of sports injuries does not go down. It’s like how cities add highway lanes and those lanes fill up, and traffic stays the same.
Right on cue, a new study looks at the past 20 years of knee injuries by professional rugby athletes in the UK and documents the same number of knee injuries now as there were 20 years ago.
The term for how freeway expansion invites more cars is “induced demand.” The increase in road capacity reduces the cost of travel, and so people with cars drive more. The analogy is not actually all that good of a fit for describing the steady rate of injuries among professional athletes. The costs related to athletes participation are not necessarily dropping. Load management and minutes restrictions have reduced playing time in some sports, while other sports, like soccer and football, have added games to teams’ calendars.
There is evidence for athletes playing harder at the highest levels of team sports. The increase in athletes’ physical demands has been well established. The economic principle that seems to be at work when it comes to athletes’ injuries is Jevons paradox.
Jevons observed that in 19th-century England technological improvements increased the efficiency of coal-burning engines, leading to more coal-burning, not less. The improved training with today’s technology reduces injury risk, but it also makes a superior, higher-performing athlete. Only a coach that did not want to win would fail to recognize and deploy the tactical opportunities that come with improving athleticism.
Technology and efficiency unlock new economic demand. New demand can lead to unintended consequences. Pollution was the byproduct of more coal; injuries are the byproduct of more athleticism. Given the continuing progress of sports training technology, the number of athlete injuries could rise even higher in the months and years ahead.
Let’s not throw away the induced demand analogy completely. Changes in US college sports have the possibility to increase the number of available paths to elite-level competition for young athletes. (Unless, of course, those changes reduce opportunities for young athletes.) If paths to the top open up, the situation can really change if there is a supply of overlooked or late-blooming talents to fill the new, expanded pipeline. Longer term, teenagers might opt to participate in real (not video game) sports.
Global soccer seems to be gaining an influx of young talent, though it is hard to tell what might have changed in the development pipeline. Graham Hunter at ESPN.com writes that a youth revolution is underway at Real Madrid, and it might be a small part of a larger European soccer youth wave.
An economic lens on athlete development and injuries fits with how privacy works, at least in terms of costs and benefits. Privacy is a cost, but with advanced privacy technology you can set privacy into a budget that organizations can manage against the utility that they sacrifice when the budget for privacy increases.


Data Sharing for Better Collaboration
My old academic advisor’s advisor is Alan Kay, a computer scientist who prototyped the user interface that became the Apple Mac and the programming language that led to object-oriented methodology. Kay famously said early in his career, “The best way to predict the future is to invent it.”

Warmenhoven et al. have a new conceptual review paper, “Unifying to Advance Understanding: Collaborative, Community‑Driven and ‘Open’ Approaches for Better Science in Sport” in Sports Medicine. Sports research needs what they’re talking about when it comes to data sharing to support advanced research and analysis for athletes’ health and performance. This paper leans towards prediction at a time when I think the world in general, and sports specifically, needs more invention.
Everyone that I talk to in sports wants fewer injuries and agrees that improved data sharing is crucial in order to realize athletes’ injury prediction and prevention. The studies to understand how, why, and what inhibit data sharing throughout high-level sports have yet to be undertaken. The answers to those research questions will go a long way towards determining the ways that computer-supported collaboration will enable researchers to work together, using datasets that are large enough to answer the most important questions.
How to improve collaboration using athletes’ data? The answer is not conceptual. Just ask the questions and try to find the answers. Do the research, and the invention will follow.
